spike.name: ML Feasibility Spike
spike.purpose: Answer one question only: can YOLOv8 detection + a simple rule-based state machine detect the six milking tasks with usable confidence from the actual camera angle? No app, no database, no UI. Throwaway code, kept isolated from the main repos.
explicit_non_goals: No final confidence thresholds — those come later from a larger labeled dataset
spike.go_no_go_question: Is there a detectable signal for each of the six tasks above noise level, and how severe is occlusion in practice at this camera angle?

structure.purpose: One-page doc: what this spike answers, how to run it, and where to record findings. Not a full project README.
Pinned deps: ultralytics, opencv-python, numpy, pandas (for results tables), matplotlib (optional, for quick visual timeline plots).
Sample video clips go here (gitignored — real footage should not be committed to source control).
Manually defined pixel coordinates for the two regions of interest (cow position 1, cow position 2) within the fixed camera frame.
Local YOLOv8 weights. Start with a pretrained/generic model to test person + object detection quality before any custom training is justified.
Runs YOLOv8 on a clip, restricted to each ROI, frame by frame. Outputs raw per-frame detections (class, bounding box, confidence) to a JSON file per clip.

Bare-bones rule-based state machine (per the spec's TASK-01..TASK-06 definitions). Consumes raw detections and produces candidate task events with a confidence score per event. This is intentionally simple — proving the concept, not the final tuned implementation.
Compares produced task_events.json against a human-labeled ground truth file for the same clip. Outputs a per-task accuracy/confidence summary and flags where occlusion likely caused misses.

Manually created by a human reviewer watching the clip: actual start/end time of each of the six tasks. This is the labeling format referenced in phase_0 of the delivery plan, tested here at small scale first.

Human-written findings after running the spike across all sample clips: does the signal exist, how bad is occlusion, which tasks are easiest/hardest to detect, and a recommendation on whether to proceed to Step 2 (walking skeleton) or revisit camera/approach first.

detections.class: person | hand | udder_region | spray_bottle | stripping_cup | teat_cups_attached | teat_cups_detached | dip_applicator

candidate_events.task_id: TASK-01 | TASK-02 | TASK-03 | TASK-04 | TASK-05 | TASK-06
candidate_events.confidence_score: float (0-1), based on detection consistency and duration over the event window
candidate_events.note: status_guess here is provisional/experimental — real thresholds are not finalized until phase_0/phase_3 of the delivery plan, using a much larger labeled dataset.

labeled_events.task_id: TASK-01 | TASK-02 | TASK-03 | TASK-04 | TASK-05 | TASK-06
labeled_events.occluded_from_camera: boolean, true if a human reviewer also could not clearly see this task from the recorded angle

per_task_summary.timing_offset_seconds: float, difference between detected and actual start time

evaluation_output_schema.overall_notes: free text — anything the reviewer noticed that the schema doesn't capture

coverage: At least one clip in suboptimal lighting if available
At least one clip where the reviewer expects occlusion to be a problem (e.g. employee crouched fully in front of udder)
minimum_sample_set_for_spike.note: This is a feasibility sample, not the full labeled dataset required later in phase_0/phase_3. Do not skip straight to large-scale labeling before this small spike gives a go/no-go signal.

steps: 1. Place sample clips in data/raw_clips/, define ROIs in data/rois.json.
2. Human reviewer creates ground_truth labels for each clip in data/ground_truth/.
3. Run detect_objects.py on each clip to produce raw detections.
4. Run state_machine.py to produce candidate task events.
5. Run evaluate.py to compare candidates against ground truth.
7. Review findings against the go/no-go criteria before starting the walking skeleton (Step 2).

proceed_to_step_2_if: Attachment/detachment events (TASK-03, TASK-05) are detected with high confidence and low timing offset — these are the least occlusion-prone and most important for session/cow-process boundaries.

At least a usable signal (even if noisy) exists for cleaning, stripping, and post-dip tasks — perfect accuracy is not required at this stage, just evidence the approach is not fundamentally blind to them.
Occlusion-flagged cases in ground truth roughly correspond to low-confidence/false-negative cases in detection — this validates that the unverifiable classification concept will work as intended.
pause_and_reconsider_if: Attachment/detachment detection itself is unreliable — this would undermine the entire session/cow-process boundary logic, not just task-level detail.
Occlusion is so severe that almost no signal exists for 3+ of the 6 tasks regardless of confidence threshold — this may mean a camera/angle change is needed before more software investment.
Detections are inconsistent across different employees in a way that suggests the model needs far more training data than initially planned.
